blocks.heading: Learn A2UI + MCP
blocks.subtext: Declarative agent UI and the protocol that carries it — five modules, each a self-contained page minted from this one payload.

blocks.text: This is a **module_map hub**: one payload whose module pages auto-encoded into their own shareable `?p=` URLs at render time. By module 3 you'll know exactly how this page was made.

modules.title: The A2UI idea
modules.description: Declarative UI for agents — payload contract, JSON in, UI out

page.text: Agents are excellent at emitting **structured data** and unreliable at writing bespoke frontend code. A2UI resolves that mismatch: the agent emits a declarative JSON payload; a renderer maps every block to a vetted, pre-built component. The UI is *data*, not code.

items.label: Type
items.text: Every block is keyed by `type` (never `component`) plus typed fields — e.g. {"type": "stat_card", "value": "99.98%", "label": "Uptime"}

items.label: Dispatch
items.text: The renderer looks up the type in its registry and calls that one renderer function with the block

items.label: Fail visible
items.text: Unknown fields are ignored; unknown types render a visible error — never a silent drop

tabs.label: What renders
tabs.code:
A stat card with an upward delta and a
progress bar — identical on every surface
that carries those atoms. No HTML written.

page.text: **Why declarative beats generated code:** a payload is reviewable at a glance, validatable against a schema before it ever renders, carries no script-injection surface, ports across surfaces unchanged, and caches like data because it *is* data. Generated HTML/JS has none of those properties.

points: A2UI = agent emits typed JSON blocks; renderer supplies all presentation
Blocks are keyed by `type`; fields are the atom's typed contract
Declarative payloads are reviewable, validatable, portable, and injection-free
The renderer's registry is the security boundary: only vetted components ever run

page.question: What does a block's `type` field do?
options: Names a CSS class applied to the block
Selects which vetted renderer function receives the block
Declares the JavaScript framework to load
page.explanation: Dispatch is the whole model: `type` looks up one pre-built, audited renderer function in the registry. The agent never ships behavior — only data.

modules.title: Atoms, catalogs & graduation
modules.description: The typed vocabulary, machine-readable discovery, and the preview → stable pipeline

page.text: An **atom** is one typed UI contract: a `type` name, documented fields, and a renderer implementation per surface. The A2UI Catalog ships 468 stable atoms — from `paragraph` to `airspace_command_deck` — grouped into **packs** (charts, effects, presentation, competition…) so a host can enable exactly the vocabulary it wants.

Discovery is machine-readable: the **ARD catalog** at `/.well-known/ai-catalog.json` lists every stable atom with its fields and surfaces, so an agent can learn the vocabulary without a human in the loop. Builder prompts are generated from the same schema — one source of truth, no drift.

Atoms have a lifecycle. **`stage: preview`** atoms live in the repo only — no published page, no catalog entry, no schema promises; they may even be deliberately field-less. **Stable** atoms get an atom page, catalog entries, schema validation, and contract tests. Promotion is enforced by CI: publish artifacts must exactly equal the stable set.

page.title: A worked example — the rocket
page.text: gdm_rocket_panel shipped as hand-curated, field-less preview content inside the MCP Apps demo page. Once proven, it graduated: typed fields (side, layer, loop), a published page, catalog entries. A fireworks animation now holds the preview slot it came from. That doorway — curated first, schema when proven — is the catalog's intake pipeline.

page.question: Does a `stage: preview` atom appear in /.well-known/ai-catalog.json?
options: Yes — everything in the repo is published
No — only stable atoms are published; preview stays repo-only
Only if it belongs to a pack
page.explanation: Every published artifact (spec.json, atom pages, the ARD catalog, builder prompts) is generated from the stable set only — and tests fail the build if they ever diverge.

points: Atom = typed contract + per-surface renderer; packs group the vocabulary
Agents discover atoms via the ARD catalog — no human docs required
preview → stable is a tested, enforced pipeline, not a convention
Curated one-off content is the intake funnel for new atoms

modules.title: Surfaces & the ?p= URL model
modules.description: One payload, eight render targets — and how a whole app fits in a link

page.text: The same payload renders on **eight surfaces**: web, Google Apps Script web apps, Meet stage, Chat, side panel, email, PDF, and MCP Apps. Each surface implements the atoms it supports; the payload doesn't change. That's the portability dividend of keeping UI as data.

items.label: Serialize
items.text: JSON → gzip (level 9, wbits 31) → URL-safe base64, padding stripped

items.label: Ship
items.text: The blob rides in the URL itself: .../exec?p=<blob> — the link IS the app, nothing stored anywhere

items.label: Decode
items.text: The renderer reverses the encoding and dispatches blocks as usual

page.text: **Ceilings, per page:** ~2,000 characters is copy-safe (terminals and chat clients truncate long lines); ~8,000 is the hard decode limit on Apps Script. A truncated `?p=` is a truncated gzip stream — it fails loudly, not partially.

items.label: Scale path A — paginate
items.text: module_map modules carrying a page: array are auto-encoded into self-contained ?p= sub-pages at render time. The page you are reading right now was minted exactly that way from the hub payload.

items.label: Scale path B — graduate
items.text: emit_deployment bakes the payload into a standing Apps Script app — no URL, no size limit, server-side named pages. Best for real, reusable content.

page.title: You are inside the example
page.text: This module opened from a hub card. No one saved this page anywhere — the renderer encoded it from the hub's payload and stamped a back-link. Depth is unbounded across pages while every individual link stays under the ceiling.

page.question: A payload encodes to 12,000 characters. What's the right move?
options: Ship it — URLs have no practical limit
Split into module_map pages, or graduate it into a standing app
page.explanation: The ~8k ceiling is per page and hard (GAS can't decode past it) — and truncating gzip destroys the stream. Pagination and graduation are the two designed scale paths.

points: One payload, eight surfaces — the payload never changes
?p= links are apps with zero storage: the URL carries the gzipped payload
~2k copy-safe, ~8k hard — per page, never truncate
Paginate (module_map) to stay shareable; graduate (emit_deployment) for no limits

modules.title: MCP fundamentals
modules.description: Hosts, servers, tools — and where the model actually sits in the loop

page.text: The **Model Context Protocol** is the open standard that connects AI applications to tools and data. Three roles: the **Host** (the chat app — claude.ai, Claude Desktop, an IDE), the **Client** (the protocol endpoint the host embeds, one per server), and the **Server** (exposes tools, resources, and prompts). Transport is **JSON-RPC 2.0** over stdio or HTTP.

items.label: initialize
items.text: Client and server exchange capabilities and protocol versions

items.label: tools/list
items.text: The server advertises its tools — name, description, JSON-Schema input contract

items.label: model decides
items.text: Tool descriptions enter the model's context; the model chooses when a tool call serves the conversation

items.label: tools/call
items.text: The host executes the call against the server and returns the result into context

page.text: **Where the model sits:** between the conversation and the tool call. The server never sees the chat; the host mediates everything — consent prompts, rate limits, result routing. A tool's JSON-Schema is a *contract*, not a suggestion: hosts validate arguments before anything executes.

cards.back: The AI application the user talks to. Owns consent, execution, and what enters the model's context.
A process exposing tools/resources/prompts over JSON-RPC. Never sees the conversation — only the calls the host forwards.
A callable capability with a name, description, and JSON-Schema input contract. The description is what the model reasons over.
Addressable content (files, data, UI templates) a server exposes by URI for hosts to read.
The wire framing: request {jsonrpc, id, method, params} → response {result | error}. Notifications omit the id.

page.question: Which party decides whether a tool call actually executes?
options: The server — it owns the tool
The model — it emitted the call
The host — it mediates consent and execution
page.explanation: The model proposes; the host disposes. Consent, validation, execution, and what flows back into context are all host responsibilities — that mediation is MCP's security model.

points: MCP = host ↔ client ↔ server over JSON-RPC 2.0
Tools are typed contracts; descriptions are what the model reasons over
The server never sees the chat — the host mediates everything
Consent and validation live at the host, by design

modules.title: MCP Apps & controlled opaqueness
modules.description: Tool results that render UI — the handshake, the sandbox, and why it matters

page.text: **MCP Apps** extends MCP so a tool result can carry an interactive UI. The server declares a UI resource; the host renders it in a **sandboxed iframe** (the *View*) and delivers data to it over a mediated postMessage channel. The model stays in the loop for tool calls — the View itself never talks to the network.

items.label: 1 · ui/initialize
items.text: The View posts ui/initialize to the Host as soon as it loads

items.label: 2 · InitializeResult
items.text: The Host replies with McpUiInitializeResult — capabilities, display modes

items.label: 3 · initialized
items.text: The View acknowledges with the ui/notifications/initialized notification

items.label: 4 · tool-result
items.text: The Host delivers the payload via ui/notifications/tool-result — the View renders it

page.text: **The sandbox is the point.** The iframe runs without `allow-same-origin`: the View genuinely cannot read or touch the host page's DOM, cookies, or storage. Every capability it has arrives through the declared message channel. For an A2UI View, the payload that arrives is declarative blocks — so even *inside* the sandbox, nothing executes that wasn't pre-vetted.

**Controlled opaqueness:** because the View is opaque to the host but its *inputs* are mediated, curated one-off content and catalog-declarative atoms can ship side by side in one iframe — same channel, same sandbox, same audit story. That is what lets new components prove themselves in production before they graduate into the typed catalog.

cards.front: What delivers the payload to the View?
cards.back: ui/notifications/tool-result, sent by the Host after the ui/initialize handshake completes.
It severs the View from the host DOM entirely — the postMessage channel becomes the only doorway, and it's mediated.
A2UI payloads are the natural cargo: declarative blocks mean the View renders vetted components, not arbitrary code — defense in depth inside the sandbox.
The host can't see into the View, but everything entering it is mediated — so curated and catalog content coexist safely, and proven pieces graduate into the schema.

points: MCP Apps = tool results that render UI in a host-sandboxed iframe
Four-message handshake: ui/initialize → result → initialized → tool-result
No allow-same-origin: the mediated channel is the View's only capability
A2UI + MCP Apps compose: declarative cargo inside a mediated sandbox — audited at both layers

blocks.text: Built with the A2UI Catalog — 468 typed atoms, rendered by the open-source Apps Script renderer this brief teaches. Browse the vocabulary at a2uicatalog.ai.
